stem:
A 5-week-old infant born at 37 weeks presents to the emergency department with 3 days of coryza, worsening work of breathing, and feeding at about a third of normal volume. The parents report fewer wet nappies in the last 12 hours and a quiet, less interactive baby.

On arrival he is alert but irritable with audible wheeze and moderate subcostal and intercostal recessions. There is no apnoea witnessed in the cubicle. Bedside viral PCR is positive for RSV. Chest x-ray shows hyperinflation only.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 168 /min, blood pressure 78/46 mmHg, respiratory rate 70 /min, SpO2 88% on room air, temperature 37.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: alert but irritable, subcostal and intercostal recessions, audible wheeze with widespread fine crackles, capillary refill 2 seconds, anterior fontanelle slightly sunken
data_table.Investigations: RSV PCR positive, chest x-ray hyperinflated lungs with no focal consolidation, venous blood gas pH 7.34 pCO2 48 mmHg HCO3 24 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial respiratory support?

options.text: Commence low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 L/min and titrate to maintain SpO2 at or above 90 percent
options.rationale: RCH bronchiolitis guidance directs low-flow nasal prong oxygen (typically 1 to 2 L/min) as the first respiratory support with an SpO2 target of 90 percent or above. High-flow is reserved for failure of low-flow after a defined trial. Starting low-flow first prevents premature escalation and the associated nursing acuity. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min with FiO2 titrated to SpO2 92 to 94 percent
options.rationale: High-flow nasal cannula is reserved for infants who fail low-flow oxygen after 1 to 2 hours of trial or who are showing severe respiratory distress. The SpO2 target in RCH guidance is 90 percent and above, not 92 to 94 percent, so starting at HFNC with a tighter target overshoots the cascade. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg with ipratropium bromide 250 micrograms and reassess in 20 minutes
options.rationale: Inhaled bronchodilators are not recommended in bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months because the wheeze is from airway oedema and debris, not bronchospasm. Trials show no clinically meaningful improvement and risk of tachycardia masking deterioration. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Give nebulised 3 percent hypertonic saline 4 mL and reassess respiratory effort in 1 hour
options.rationale: Nebulised hypertonic saline is not recommended in RCH guidance for routine bronchiolitis management; the limited evidence does not support meaningful reduction in admission length, and it can trigger bronchospasm. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Give oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg once daily for 3 days and observe in short-stay unit
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not indicated in bronchiolitis. Cochrane and RCH guidance both find no benefit on admission rate or length of stay, and there is concern about prolonged viral shedding. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Initial oxygen support in moderate bronchiolitis is low-flow nasal prong oxygen titrated to SpO2 90 percent or above, with high-flow reserved for failure of low-flow after a trial period. Bronchodilators, hypertonic saline, and corticosteroids do not change outcomes in typical bronchiolitis under 12 months. The candidate must resist defaulting to the most resource-intensive option early.
key_points: RCH SpO2 target in bronchiolitis is at or above 90 percent, not 92 to 94 percent.
Low-flow oxygen first, HFNC after 1 to 2 hours of failed low-flow.
No role for salbutamol or ipratropium under 12 months in bronchiolitis.
Steroids and hypertonic saline are not recommended.
explanation.pearls: Low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 to 2 L/min titrated to SpO2 at or above 90 percent is first-line in bronchiolitis under RCH CPG; reassess at 1 to 2 hours before escalating to HFNC.

sources.label: Cochrane review of bronchodilators in bronchiolitis
